concepts.lesson: Light is a form of energy that enables us to see objects. It travels in straight lines in a homogeneous medium and interacts with surfaces through reflection and refraction.
concepts.real_example: We can see objects in a room when light from a lamp reflects off them and enters our eyes.
key_points: Light is a form of energy
Travels in straight lines in a uniform medium

concepts.lesson: Reflection occurs when light strikes a surface and bounces back into the same medium. Smooth surfaces like mirrors produce regular reflection, forming clear images.
concepts.real_example: Seeing your image in a bathroom mirror is due to reflection of light.

concepts.lesson: Reflection follows two laws: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and the incident ray, reflected ray, and the normal at the point of incidence lie in the same plane.
concepts.real_example: A ray of light hitting a mirror at 30° reflects at the same angle on the other side of the normal.
key_points: Angle of incidence equals angle of reflection
Incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in same plane

concepts.lesson: Spherical mirrors are mirrors whose reflecting surface is part of a sphere. They are of two types: concave mirrors and convex mirrors.
concepts.real_example: Shaving mirrors are concave mirrors while rear-view mirrors in vehicles are convex mirrors.

concepts.lesson: Concave mirrors can form real or virtual images depending on the position of the object relative to the focus and centre of curvature. Real images are inverted while virtual images are erect.
concepts.real_example: A concave mirror used by dentists produces a magnified upright image when the object is close to the mirror.
key_points: Can form real or virtual images

concepts.lesson: Convex mirrors always form virtual, erect, and diminished images regardless of the object's position. They provide a wider field of view.
concepts.real_example: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles to see a larger area behind.
key_points: Image is always virtual and erect
Image is smaller than the object

concepts.lesson: The mirror formula relates object distance, image distance, and focal length. Magnification describes the size of the image compared to the object.
concepts.real_example: Using the mirror formula helps calculate where an image will form in a concave mirror used in headlights.

concepts.lesson: Refraction is the bending of light when it passes from one medium to another due to change in its speed. The amount of bending depends on the refractive index of the medium.
concepts.real_example: A pencil partially immersed in water appears bent due to refraction.
key_points: Bending of light at boundary of media

concepts.lesson: The refractive index of a medium measures how much light slows down in that medium compared to vacuum. It determines how much light bends during refraction.
concepts.real_example: Light bends more in glass than in air because glass has a higher refractive index.
key_points: Higher refractive index means slower light speed

concepts.lesson: Due to refraction, objects under water appear closer to the surface than they actually are. This phenomenon is called apparent depth.
concepts.real_example: The bottom of a swimming pool appears shallower than its real depth.
